Not very ideal when the zombie apocalypse arrives, to say the least and when taking the price in account, this is completely unacceptable. Hopefully this comment has been at least somewhat helpful. Let this serve as a warning for anyone looking into Ecoflow products. //Simon

author: @alisonmurray7234
text:
Last month I decided to go with the Delta mini for blackout management. Discovered the hard way with multi days outage in Arwen that there are things I need to have....I'm disabled and need heating / hot water for various reasons, and easy to use when I'm really poorly.   Impressed so far.
Also got some USB rechargeable lamps etc which gives light and had a simple single burner gas stove for cooking. So, government do your worst.
The Delta will power the electric blanket,

 microwave and coffee machine and even  hairdryer (!) with the XBoost function...but be careful of this if the appliance is sensitive to voltage drops! 
Sadly Xboost on/ off is only controllable with  the app....and its the app thats given me the most grief because it doesn't like the way I have my BT router configured (standard out if the box!!!) 
The other issue I've found with a non ecoflow solar panel ( only 120W portable bought fir another device......) is that once its settled on an input wattage, it doesn't increase it.
So if your set up catches a few Watts in the early morning sun, say 25W, the Delta will continue to charge at 25W, even if the array starts to produce more power! Unplugged the solar panel, plugged back in and at midday input jumped to 89W! Lots of wasted solar ☹️
Does this happen with their own solar panel I wonder? Would love you to test that for me....pennies are a bit tight to just buy one on the off-chance!

author: @EcoFlowTech
text: Hey Robert! Quite appreciate your informative introduction of DELTA 2 on all the specs and features!! We do choose LFP batteries for better performance and more safety. Plus pairing it or other EcoFlow products with solar panels is definitely a cost-effective way to power essentials. We look forward to your other wonderful videos and hope that everyone will be accessible to portable and affordable electricity. ❤

author: @RiverMersey
text:
Could I make a request of EcoFlow?
Design the electronics in such a way that, it can be charged from the mains, however when the grid mains is absent, the same mains input could become a mains output. Doing this would mean the house electrics could be powered from this device. Certain safeties would be needed so that the plug isn't live when disconnected, and when the grid mains returns it can revert to being charged again.

author: @jamest.5001
text: 3000 cycles to 80% capacity, it will still work after the 2300-3000 cycles,  probably about 3000 more cycles, to 50-60% capacity,   although failures are possible at that age,  it's best to only charge it if you are going to use it! And not keep it fully charged, if it's going to be used for emergency backup, maybe keep it at 80-90 % charge. And fully charge before use, if possible, it will make it last longer!

replies.author: @wolin289
replies.text: I also live in Central FL and chose not to have a generator and didn't even consider a ham radio.  We were out of electricity for 7 days with Ian.  The expense of the generator as well as the gas you have to keep on hand wasn't feasible.  Also your car is a generator.  We charged phones in the car. Instead we have battery op fans (one for each person) and alternative ways to cook and bake.  Except for not having internet access, we really didn't miss electricity that much.  My neighbor with solar panels had them crushed from tree limbs.  With all the maintenance one has to do with those things, it's better to just go low tech for a time, and shortly services will be restored.

replies.author: @tj48649
replies.text: @wolin289 The saving of the food in my freezer was worth every single penny of the generator and the cost to run it (only a 3-4 hours a day, btw.  You don't run them 24/7.) A car is not a 'generator'.  It cannot run the necessities in your home - mine can't.  You gripe about the cost to run the generator yet you ran your car to charge your phone? And, lastly, your neighbor is a poor example.  What % of people who had solar panels did had theirs crushed by trees do you think?  1% maybe; at the max 10%.  That means you're making your decision based on poor odds.  I'd rethink my logic if I were you.

author: @kirtodam6540
text: The app allows you to turn on or off the individual ports, and you can change the charging wattage. If you are charging from another battery bank with a small inverter this is very handy. It also shows in more detail the input and output. It is useful to have as you can move the solar panel while looking at your phone to see where the optimum angle is for example.  I have tried to charge mine from a 400w Vevor (12/24v) wind turbine and it does not like it as it needs 11v minimum to charge and my turbine was not at that or above for long enough. I have the Delta max 2016kwh and absolutely love it and think that even if you don't use it every day like I do, every one should have one as a back up.

author: @MyProjectBoxChannel
text: Now all you need to do is make one of those "Suicide" extension leads, with the  plug on each end. Turn off the main isolator in your fuse board, and a few unnecessary power hungry circuits. Plug your "dangerous extension of death", into a spare socket outlet(back feeding power through a socket). Then feed the other end into your eco-flow unit. That way a few sockets in your house, and all the lighting should work. As long as you don't overload the unit of course.

author: @arkwrightnut
text: Hi I have just bought the Ecoflow Delta Max myself and am in the process of testing it on various appliances in my home. with encouraging results, it will even power the induction hobs of my Everhot cooker for emergency cooking etc, but I have plugged my Renogy solar panel to its input ( which I use to float charge my car battery via a Victron mppt controller) and although the specs say 11 to 100v solar input it doesn't start inputting until the panel is in bright sunlight ( approx 50 to 80watts indicated) whereas the Victron will provide around 0.5 to 1amp even on cloudy days. I have ordered a second PV panel to wire in series with the existing one in hopes that it will increase the open circuit volts so it with charge the Ecoflow on cloudy days. I would be interested to here your comments. Regards Chris J.

author: @misamsung6191
text: Narrowboaters use these or similar ones to prop up their domestic batteries.  They put these on their appliances that use the most electricity (washers, tumble driers, fridges etc) and use the solar panel to recharge them.  It allows them to not drain their domestic batteries, or run their engine if they are moored up for a few days.  I looked into one but a petrol powered generator was MUCH cheaper as a backup power source.

author: @davidsoulsby1102
text:
A nice bit of kit if a little expensive.
Some extra things to think about.
Charging it via the solar panel, nope certainly not from flat, the included panel will top up but it will take days to charge even with full sun.
Yes wind will also work with the same caveat as sola and you need a voltage regulator to keep the wind at the stated voltage or it will just cut off the charging.
Its uses, its meant as an off grid power source for working not as a backup supply, it can be used as a backup but recharging is always going to be slow when off grid.
The max rating of 10a means it will not cope with Cookers, Electric central heating, high power showers, heat driers, some washing machines.
And that's not taking into account you will wants other electronics working at the same time.
Think of it as a supply for fridge, freezer, lights, gas central heating, kettles and one ring hob or grill. 
The more high load devices the less it lasts.
Now here is the real issue.... its not designed to power a property, that means you would have to bodge up a lead with two plugs on. very dangerous in uneducated hands. I have been unable to check if it has automatic cut off when the grid supply comes back on, again a potential hazard. it is not designed to isolate the grid connection when running which will lead to energizing the grid and potential fatal outcomes for electrical supply staff. If you want to plug into your home sockets you have to isolate the grid supply and that is not as simple as switching off the main switch, you need to guarantee the isolation is physical or you may kill someone and are certainly breaking the law.
The best way to use this is run extension leads to your appliances for them to be plugged into.
Or get a qualified electrician to instal a change over switch which complies with regulations.
If you backfeed onto the network the companies will prosecute you regardless of any injuries. Electricity will go any direction but the protection, like circuit breakers don't work backwards.
I already have grid tie solar and backup batteries and worked many years for an electricity supplier so I know what it's like to be on the wrong end of the wiggly stuff.
Cheers.

author: @Isabella-lq1qs
text:
What a terrific video. I bought the predecessor of this machine back in April. It had a crack in the body by the display when it arrived. Put me in a bit of a panic as to how it could have happened. It was well packaged and there was nothing floating around in the packaging that could have caused such a crack. I thought it must be something inside the case had put pressure and caused the crack. As it happens Ecoflow customer service was very prompt and polite in response to my email. After sending some photos of the damage the unit was quickly exchanged without expense to me. I call that excellent after sales service. 
Having now seen the innards of the unit it's hard to say what could have caused the crack. Just a weakness in the plastic that got missed , I guess. 
Not looking forward to the possible blackouts but not as fearful as I might have been, I expect this unit will be a godsend. I have also picked up a freezer alarm just in case the blackouts happen at unexpected times. Good luck everyone and stay safe. 😃 🇬🇧

author: @kevinforget549
text: ecoflow has the right idea but there's much cheaper options out there if you want to put a few premade parts together. just buying a 12 volt lifepo4 battery, 1000 watt inverter, mppt charge controller and two 100 watt solar panels will run you almost half what this costs and if there's no sun you can use the inverter on your car and use it to charge up the battery. another nice idea is using that setup with an ebike as an emergency generator(3 phase rectifiers are cheap on amazon). hook the ebike output(after rectification) to the mppt and it will change the variable voltage into a stable charge voltage for your battery.

author: @bartronicsecurity
text: It will take many hours to charge up from a car socket.  Remember car accessory sockets have a 10 amp fuse.  So 10 x 13.5 volts is only 135 watts. So to charge up a 1kw battery assuming 100% conversion efficiency with 135watts will be over 7 hours. Same with the small solar panel. Let's not even go to the 3kw big version.  Pretty much a large ups in a nutshell.

author: @stephenbrough8132
text:
Trying to remember what we did in the 70's when powercuts were extremely common... Oh yeah... nothing.   Then the power came back on and we still did nothing.  There was nothing to do in the 70's.   It was actually THE most exiting thing that happened most weeks.    Taking bets on power returning ... 3 - 2 - 1 .....  wrong again.       

I'm sure most (older) people don;t give a shiny sh if the power goes off for a few hours - what is there to miss?   The internet?   In that case they're PROBABLY doing us a favour  - like running out of fags and deciding to pack in instead of fretting about how we'll possibly cope without coughing our guts up every day.  

I only worry about struggling parents with tiny children or babies - changing nappies by candle light sounds a bit of a bummer.

author: @tobiwan2741
text:
I have the Delta 2. One thing that doesn't get mentioned in the video is it's excellent charging performance from both the AC (1200W) and via Solar MPPT (500W) while not having the awkward voltage and current limitation on the solar input the new Anker LFP has (basically means you have to pay for Ankers overpriced panels or loose a lot of charge performance)
This charge rate makes it much more practical in a rolling blackout situation. You can charge it from AC when power is running or from a generator in just 1 hour.
The app is actually quite practical. You might have this unit setup somewhere away from where you are (where the solar panel or the generator is - and your ears aren't - see below) and the app allows you to easily monitor charge and control the outputs.
There is only 2 negatives with this unit: 
An adjustable DC output would have been nice (voltage and current) so you could use it to charge or use all the appliances which use a DC power brick (using the brick will waste alot of energy in the inverter and brick compared to a direct DC output) or directly charge other batteries.
The other thing is that if you plan on using it as an UPS, even at moderate load, the fan noise is quite loud.

author: @aaronfranklin324
text:
Interesting. Do people understand that a few won't-start-a-car-anymore junkyard batteries, a voltmeter, and a 15 quid inverter can do everything that this device promises, but without all those gimmick, designed to fail electronics and expensive  patented batteries?.
If you wanted to take it further you'd take the Tri- generation route, which triples energy harvest from solar or wind or ambient temperature difference, and use hot and cold stores: Murray gas demonstrated great options here, like Zeolite clays, Urea, Calcium chloride....
Salt Ice is great for cold storage.
And use an organic rankine cycle turbine, grid coupled via a synchronous AC motor/ generator. No batteries required. Just a valve adjusting gas flow to the turbine to suit demand, dispose of excess energy to the grid. 
You can even configure to use grid or other electric power to charge your hot and cold stores by using a Tesla turbine in dual mode and operating it as a refrigeration pump.

author: @jamesross1003
text: Very good product sounds like.  The fact that it is not entirely locked in and upgradable is wonderful.  You mentioned refrigeration earlier in the video as an issue in blackouts.  It doesn't have to be though.  Make a refrigeration unit that works from the heat of let's say your rocket stove.  Use the heat from the stove to pressurize a refrigeration unit that will cool as the pressure comes down.  My grandfather's RV had a refrigerator/freezer that ran on LP gas.  So it is not that difficult to be done.  There is also a unit an experimental engineer made to run from the sun's heat that would freeze water during the overnight period.  Easy enough to google that one and use a similar set up to make a rocket stove version.  Love these kinds of things Rob!  Keep pumping out those videos!

author: @scotttovey
text:
Why have an app on your cell phone to manage the power backup?

That's not a hard thing to figure.

All we need do is take into consideration the recent lockout of smart meters in Colorado, that prevented the 2000 Electric customers affected by the lockout, from accessing and utilizing electric power during a 90 degree heat wave. 

The purpose of an app on your smart phone is to insure that they can send a command to your power supply via your smart phone, and make your power supply non functional at the very point in time when you need it the most.

Yeah, I know that sounds like a conspiracy theory, but let's be honest about it; this current energy crisis is man made beginning with the policies of the marxist left, enemies of the United States, within the United States, choosing to shut down the US petroleum and natural gas industry.

author: @offgridwanabe
text: I have had a Humless 1500 watt "solar generator" for 10 years so nothing new here but it is very handy during blackouts makes coffee and runs my fridge for 4 hours or more and it can be used as a ups if desired. Charging is dead simple as long as you follow the voltage specifications so I see a windmill charger in your future. Also a great tool for camping. Most people find out that it can be used every day to offset your power bill but the drawback is the battery may be low when it comes to the black out lol. Of course now I have a large solar system it mostly sits under my desk but I can use it when needed.

replies.author: @DerekRoss1958
replies.text:
They do have that mass. When Hurricane Dorian came through Nova Scotia a few years ago, I prepared for it by packing the chest freezer with water bottles. It took eight days before power was restored to my house (No generator at the time) but the contents of the freezer remained frozen throughout. 

So as long as you don't open the freezer door, a day without power shouldn't matter too much. Particularly if you fill any spare space in the freezer with water bottles.

author: @Liowen
text: Lost power this weekend for nearly a full day (wind took out a powerline) but thankfully we had a generator that is decent on gas, about 5 gallons will get about 9 hours.  I had my fridge, deep freezer, 2 TV's, land line (which was out also), PC, and modem all running on it and the generator never missed a beat.  At some point (IE when I have more disposable income) I am going to look into getting a full battery backup system so I can run my well, water heater, and furnace as well, luckily it wasn't too cold this weekend but I have a round kerosene heater as a backup heat supply if needed.  Lucky for me also is that my stove is propane and has continuous pilots that don't require igniters, so I could boil up water on the  stove for hot water for dishes and such.

author: @1upPaintingAB
text:
Knew these last day events were coming a few years ago. I bought some much cheaper options due to budget but anything is better than nothing.
A simple small battery bank, solar charge controller, and a foldable panel does just fine if you can accomplish basic wiring. Then you just add what you need (inverter which is the biggest cost, 12v sockets, 5v usb, etc)
These all-in-one type units are good for what they are though, especially for those who don't care for wiring or setting things up. 
I have a 280AH one that does the trick for most things and it's very portable.

author: @janemalins3092
text: Is it correct that in order to store these correctly you must not exceed a 50% charge ?

replies.author: @PsiQ
replies.text:
... its just import regulations made up by different countries.
If you ship a Taycan full electric to china it needs to be almost empty. Usually lithium cell items get shipped between 30 to 50% charged, because producer doesnt know how long its going to stay on the shelves.
The uppermost charhe is also bad if something happens and also charging everything to max costs money if the numbers are high.

Meanwell produces a special power supply for charging and feeding back power into the power grid. Scalable for battery (car) manufacturing and testing, since they have to test capacity but again dont/wont ship fully charged. You normally did not get your car with a full tank either ;-)

author: @peterrockell1527
text: I've been thinking about these (both ecoFlow and Bluetti), they would keep my house basically heated and lit for a few hours (with the grid turned off, of course),  not that I'd attempt to use the electric cooker. But it all comes down to a lot of money for something that would get very little use in my case, so I've gone for the more expensive and longer term ESS solution, which probably won't get installed until spring. Ah well, the blackouts might not happen and then should only be for a few hours at a time.

author: @richardsandwell2285
text: These are plug & play and a great idea for those with limited land and for people who have limited electrical knowledge. The biggest problem people will have is separating their home incoming power and applying this to your house safely without blowing the thing up or causing injury. Years ago my mum was cooking a Turkey in her gas oven on Xmas day, a power cut lasting several hours ensued. The gas oven, central heating and lighting went down straight away, despite being gas the ovens electric gas valve closed, similar the central heating and pump failed to work. I had a small inverter and a battery, I made sure the main incoming supply was fully isolated and then made up a suicide lead with a 13 Amp plug on the inverters outlet, which I then back fed into the mains through a plug socket which I made sure no one touched.  It was not the correct way to do things, a proper change over switch needs to be fitted but in an emergency it was the only option, we had heating xmas day and the Turkey continued to cook till completion.

author: @Laser2120
text: Looks like a really neat thing but I could never justify spending that much on it.

author: @JSabh
text: I appreciate the product being available because it does help some. Still, my house runs on solar and battery with a gas generator so I understand the world of solar and batteries quiet well. It is very easy to do in my opinion to build your own and much cheaper. Besides, you want to know how your power system works as well as have redundancy built in with multiple inverters, battery bank ect.

Obviously I am bound by an NDA. But if anyone I know would appreciate the technology it would be you. You love pushing that envelope as do I and Ecoflow as well.

@user-vm6mw6du8m 
"What about carbon monoxide in a van or closed environment?"

That's quite simple; you don't use an energy source that produces a lot of carbon monoxide.
Go with propane or an alcohol heater.

But the pipes that pass through it will circulate air on the interior. I'm working on building it based on the veg oil burners

author: @Endofdays73
text: Chinese company so take things like build ,longevity and warranty with pinch of salt

replies.author: @TheWatchmanNews
replies.text: You show me an American or UK company that makes these and I will show you a product that was assembled in the US or UK but still made...... in China.

replies.author: @Endofdays73
replies.text: @TheWatchmanNews true but you said built in uk or us  warranty would be honoured i have 3 Chinese inverters here from 3 different company's 2 year warranty 2 DOA one lasted a week at half rated load went up in shower of sparks not one of company's ever replied to warranty requests

replies.author: @TheWatchmanNews
replies.text: @Endofdays73 tell that to all the owners of high dollar Brinkmann grills... It matters who you buy from it really does. When it comes to power supplies they can be made cheap or they can be made well. The way to physically tell the difference without opening it up is weight. Go find 2 computer power supplies. Same wattage but one from a fly by night company and the other say an Antec. Weigh them. The Antec will weigh much more. It has the better components. I bet your inverters are like my cheap inverter and weigh very little. Now go buy an Aims inverter and tell me the difference in how it lasts. Both built where? Exactly. It matters who you buy from.

replies.author: @Endofdays73
replies.text: @TheWatchmanNews i am not a noob 30+ years in mechanical and electrical fields i purchased them to show a friend that would believe me  that quality is something your need when dealing with anything you rely on and having warranty from manufacture that is valid  . he got the point

replies.author: @TheWatchmanNews
replies.text: @Endofdays73 right on same here. Just turned 50 and I completed my first electrical circuit on my own at the age of 5. To be honest it is simply an unstable buyers market. Its not about where its made its about how its made and from what parts. I just scrapped a monitor that had some of the bad capacitors in it. All brands had those bad caps. They were the only caps available for purchase for a period of time. Now those bad caps are still showing up in products but not from the reputable companies. Still all Chinese made. Its an epidemic that all our nations need to fix. We were sold out for bigger profit margins. You are correct though a good warranty is important. I cant argue that a bit. From what I have seen companies like Ecoflow and DJI honor their warranties.

author: @William_Hada
text: Interesting that you are reviewing this because last month I bought the Ecoflow Delta pro package with  battery, generator, and solar panel, 3600w. I was also very impressed with it's versatility and build quality. The gas powered generator is surprisingly quiet as advertised. It is was not cheap but worth the peace of my mind it gives me about possible power shortages and outages this coming winter.

author: @simonhodges6084
text: Then again you can get a 3KW open frame petrol generator from Screwfix for £329 leaving you plenty of money for fuel out of that £1600 budget.  Maybe you could do a video showing people the most effective way of implementing a petrol generator solution in a house?

replies.author: @richardsandwell2285
replies.text: Its a fair comment, but Petrol generators are rubbish really, I have one, I took it to work and left it there, they are a total pain, they make a noise so loud it would wake the dead and certainly pee the neighbours off, mine refuses to run my washing machine properly my 3KW inverter and lead acid batts have no trouble. Now here is the big problem, modern petrol contains Ethanol and to make it mix with the long chain oily hydrocarbons chemicals have to be added, giving the petrol a safe shelf life of about six months before it gums up the carburettor with nasty pink jelly. You can go to farming pro gardening shops and buy a special petrol which will sit in your genny for at least five years without degradation but £20 for 5 litres.

replies.author: @D-B-Cooper
replies.text: If you buy a good one it will use less than half the petrol and be so quiet that you can’t hear it. I can buy premium that doesn’t have ethanol. Between my chainsaw, outboard and generator I can cycle through my gas before it goes bad with stabilizer in it.

replies.author: @justinweatherford8129
replies.text: So, you would rather pay more in maintenance costs and fueling? A solar generator may be more expensive at the beginning, but a lot cheaper in the long term.

replies.author: @RonnieRubble

It will run my fridge and freezer for a few days but my generator is for longer outages. We have winter stores that knock power out.

author: @Route72Trucks
text:
I wonder if you could give me your opinion on a thought experiment.  

One or two solar panels powering a (hot water type) heating coil immersed in hydronic oil / mineral oil….  Surrounded by a large mass of sand.  

Allowing the sun to charge your sand battery.  

You are brilliant and your thoughts would be greatly appreciated.

replies.author: @justinweatherford8129
replies.text:
You haven’t mentioned, so I figured that I would state the obvious. You should also have piping to extract the stored heat for later use.
I also believe that using plant based oils might also be better for the planet.

replies.author: @Route72Trucks
@justinweatherford8129
replies.text:
@Route72Trucks the benefits of using water as a heat transfer medium is that it is more than environmentally friendly to use and it is easier in most cases to get ahold of.
The downsides of using water as the transfer medium is that it has a low boiling point and it will likely have contaminates which will clog the system in the long term.

replies.author: @Route72Trucks
replies.text:
@justinweatherford8129  I use a wood boiler to heat my home.  It’s very easy to over heat water and in the wrong situation becomes explosive.  

Water is a no go.   

A non flowing mass of oil (whatever variety) heated to a max temp reasonably allowed.  Surrounded by a large mass of sand to insulate the oil and act as the medium to transfer the heat into the space around it. 

Like a heated rock in your living room.
